{"ai": "Canva has implemented a reverse image search system to help maintain a high-quality image library for its users. This system is used in the Template Assistant, which is the menu shown to Canva's template designers. The system works by using image embeddings, which are high-dimensionality vectors extracted from machine learning models trained on image-related tasks. Canva tested several models, including DINOv2, CLIP, ViTMAE, DreamSim, and CaiT, and found that DINOv2 produced the most suitable results for their use case. The system is integrated into the Template Assistant, where a design is linted for violating media and highlighted, and the top 8 similar images are displayed to designers. Upon selection, similar images are swapped out with the image in the frame. The system is also part of a human-in-the-loop system for quality control. Initial pilots by professional designers showed a 4.5x increase in the speed of image replacement when using the replacement suggestions compared to a regular search. Canva is also looking to improve the system in the future, such as by detecting any symbols or text in an image before storing it in the database and recording these as metadata fields in the vector database."}

{
  "title": "Automatically Highlighting Car Attributes from Video Using Probabilistic Classifiers",
  "abstract": "This paper presents a proof of concept for a method that automatically highlights a car's attributes from a video. The approach uses probabilistic classifiers to predict attributes such as car type, brand, number of doors, and number of seats, as well as the orientation of the car with respect to the camera. The certainty of the predictions is used as a measure of the quality of the image in depicting the corresponding attribute. The method selects frames from a video that yield the highest certainty for each attribute and uses the orientation predictor to extract frames that show the object from different angles. The approach has potential applications in e-commerce, where it can be used to automatically generate product images that highlight key attributes of a product.",
  "introduction": "In e-commerce, it is often necessary to generate product images that highlight key attributes of a product. For cars, this can include attributes such as type, brand, number of doors, and number of seats. Traditionally, this has been done manually, which is time-consuming and error-prone. In this paper, we present a proof of concept for a method that automatically highlights a car's attributes from a video. The approach uses probabilistic classifiers to predict attributes and uses the certainty of the predictions as a measure of the quality of the image in depicting the corresponding attribute.",
  "methodology": {
    "problem_definition": "The goal is to automatically highlight a car's attributes from a video. This involves predicting attributes such as car type, brand, number of doors, and number of seats, as well as the orientation of the car with respect to the camera.",
    "approach": "We use probabilistic classifiers to predict attributes and use the certainty of the predictions as a measure of the quality of the image in depicting the corresponding attribute. The method selects frames from a video that yield the highest certainty for each attribute and uses the orientation predictor to extract frames that show the object from different angles.",
    "implementation": "We train a model to predict attributes such as car type, brand, number of doors, and number of seats. The model can also predict the orientation of the front of the car with respect to the camera. We use a probabilistic classifier with multiple predictions, one per attribute, on top of a feature extractor. In particular, we use InceptionResnetV5 pre-trained on ImageNet as feature extractor for our model. The probabilistic nature of our classifier allows us to obtain a certainty measure for each attribute's predictions. We train each attribute predictor separately via transfer learning by freezing the feature extractor and the layers corresponding to the other attributes' predictions.",
    "data": "We use two datasets: PASCAL3D+ and CompCars. The former is used to train a predictor for the car's orientation. The latter is used for training the rest of the predictors.",
    "results": "We have built a proof of concept of a method that can automatically highlight a car's attributes from a video. The method selects frames from a video that yield the highest certainty for each attribute and uses the orientation predictor to extract frames that show the object from different angles. The approach has potential applications in e-commerce, where it can be used to automatically generate product images that highlight key attributes of a product."
  },
  "discussion": "The approach has potential applications in e-commerce, where it can be used to automatically generate product images that highlight key attributes of a product. However, there are still some challenges to overcome for taking this simple idea to the next level. We need to find ways to efficiently train classifiers to predict any object's attributes, not only for cars, without relying on labelled datasets. One such option is to use unsupervised methods to highlight objects' visual attributes without the need for large amounts of labels. We shall harness the information about the temporal correlation of frames. Right now, the predictions for the video are obtained by processing frames individually without any attention to the valuable temporal information. Further improvements shall include the addition of different neural network architectures for handling a sequence of images. We shall investigate the trade-off between the computational complexity of the model and its accuracy. Using a larger neural network as a feature extractor can achieve better results but require significantly more computational resources.",
  "conclusion": "In this paper, we have presented a proof of concept for a method that automatically highlights a car's attributes from a video. The approach uses probabilistic classifiers to predict attributes and uses the certainty of the predictions as a measure of the quality of the image in depicting the corresponding attribute. The method selects frames from a video that yield the highest certainty for each attribute and uses the orientation predictor to extract frames that show the object from different angles. The approach has potential applications in e-commerce, where it can be used to automatically generate product images that highlight key attributes of a product.",

{
  "ai_analysis": {
    "summary": "The article discusses the implementation of query-based personalized recommendations at Stitch Fix, focusing on how images from clients are used as queries to recommend items that are both relevant to the image and personalized to the client. The process involves computer vision to identify clothing items in images, mapping them to anchor items in the catalog, and using latent spaces to compute similarity and affinity scores. A weighted combination of distance and affinity is used to rank items, ensuring both relevance and personalization. The solution leverages multiple teams' work, including latent style representation, personalization, and similarity models.",
    "key_points": [
      "Query-based recommendations use client-submitted images as queries to recommend items.",
      "Computer vision is used to detect clothing items in images and map them to anchor items in the catalog.",
      "Latent spaces are used to represent items and clients, enabling similarity and affinity scoring.",
      "A weighted combination of distance (from anchor items) and affinity (from client preferences) is used to rank items.",
      "Diversity in recommendations is ensured by selecting top-N items per category based on the multi-objective score.",
      "The solution integrates multiple models and teams' work, including latent style, personalization, and similarity models."
    ],
    "technical_terms": [
      "Latent style space",
      "Anchor items",
      "Hybrid space (H)",
      "Client space (C)",
      "Multi-objective ranking",
      "k-nearest neighbors (k-NN)",
      "Affinity scores",
      "Distance measures",
      "FAISS (for similarity search)",
      "Interleaving design"
    ],
    "team_involvement": [
      "Core Representation Learning Team (Latent Style space)",
      "Styling Recommendations Team (Personalization affinity scores)",
      "Shop Recommendations Team (Image and item similarity services)",
      "Client Recommendations Team (Overall work)"
    ],
    "references": [
      "FAISS (for similarity search in latent spaces)",
      "Category theory (for composition in multi-objective ranking)",
      "Pareto frontiers (for multi-objective problems)",
      "Interleaving design (for learning the weighting parameter α)"
    ],
    "conclusion": "The article highlights Stitch Fix's approach to query-based personalized recommendations, emphasizing the integration of multiple models and spaces to ensure both relevance and personalization. The solution is scalable and leverages existing investments in latent representation learning, personalization, and similarity models."
  }
}

{
  "title": "Implementing the Temporal Fusion Transformer for Accurate Demand Forecasting at Picnic",
  "content": "At Picnic, we are constantly striving to improve our operations and deliver the best possible experience for our customers. One of the key challenges we face is accurately forecasting demand for our products. To address this, we have implemented the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), a state-of-the-art model specifically designed for time-series forecasting.\n\nThe TFT model is a transformer-based neural network that allows us to predict demand at both the article and delivery levels. It supports a wide range of input types, including temporal and static features, and provides explainability through its variable selection mechanism. This enables us to understand which features are most important for our predictions and to interpret the model's behavior.\n\nImplementing the TFT model has not been without its challenges. We faced issues with memory usage and computational power, as the model requires significant resources to train. However, the benefits have been well worth the effort. We have seen a 20% increase in our forecast precision, particularly in slow-moving articles, which are the most difficult to forecast. The model's explainability has also helped us communicate its behavior to our stakeholders and guide our model development efforts.\n\nOverall, we are very pleased with the results of implementing the TFT model. It has allowed us to make more accurate predictions and to better manage our inventory. We are excited to continue exploring new models and techniques to further improve our demand forecasting capabilities.",
  "summary": "Picnic has successfully implemented the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) model to improve demand forecasting accuracy. The model has provided a 20% increase in forecast precision and has enabled better inventory management. Despite the challenges of implementing the model, the benefits have been well worth the effort.",
  "key_takeaways": [
    "Implementing a complex model like TFT requires investing time to grasp its intricacies and tailor it to your needs.",
    "Deep learning models like TFT demand significant hardware resources (GPUs), which can increase production costs.",
    "Start with a strong baseline, potentially utilising simpler models like gradient boosting.",
    "The landscape of machine learning evolves rapidly, with newer models like TiDe emerging."
  ]
}
